page: Amazon Web Services m4.xlarge is a general-purpose virtual machine instance running on the Xen hypervisor. It features an x86_64 architecture powered by an Intel Xeon E5-2686 v4 processor operating at 2.4 GHz. The instance provides 4 dedicated vCPUs derived from 2 physical cores with hyper-threading enabled. It is equipped with 16.0 GB of system memory, yielding 4.0 GB of RAM per core. No local storage or GPU hardware is bundled with this instance type, and it features a baseline network bandwidth of 1 Gbps.

Benchmark data places the performance of the m4.xlarge in the lower tiers relative to modern hardware. In CPU testing, the stress-ng single-core and multi-core benchmarks scored 1282.39 and 3353.46 respectively, both falling into the bottom 25% category. Similarly, the Geekbench single-core score of 976.0 and multi-core score of 2156.0 are categorized as weak. Memory bandwidth tests show 177.74 GB/sec for cached small blocks and 24.01 GB/sec for uncached large blocks, indicating limited throughput. Database operations under Passmark scored 1244.1, and Redis SET operations achieved 1.44 million operations per second, which is in the bottom 10%.

Given its older generation Intel Xeon architecture and weak benchmark results across computational, memory, and database tests, the m4.xlarge represents a legacy resource tier. It offers a balanced 1:4 vCPU-to-RAM ratio, which provides resource density suitable for basic utility workloads rather than performance-critical applications. The lack of local storage requires reliance on network-attached storage, which, combined with the 1 Gbps baseline network speed, limits high-throughput data processing. This instance is suited for low-traffic web serving, basic background utility tasks, or legacy applications that do not require high-performance computing or modern instruction sets.
description: Amazon Web Services m4.xlarge is a general-purpose instance utilizing the Xen hypervisor and an x86_64 Intel Xeon E5-2686 v4 processor running at 2.4 GHz. It features 4 dedicated vCPUs, 16.0 GB of RAM, and a baseline network bandwidth of 1 Gbps, with no local storage or GPU accelerators. Performance benchmarks consistently place this instance in the bottom 25% tier, with a Geekbench single-core score of 976.0 and a multi-core score of 2156.0. Memory bandwidth is also limited, reaching 24.01 GB/sec for uncached large blocks. Due to these hardware constraints and lower relative performance, the m4.xlarge is suited for legacy applications, low-traffic web serving, and basic utility tasks that require a balanced vCPU-to-memory ratio without high computational or storage throughput demands.
og_description: Amazon Web Services m4.xlarge is a general-purpose instance featuring 4 vCPUs, 16 GB RAM, and an Intel Xeon E5-2686 v4 CPU, suitable for low-demand legacy workloads and basic web serving.
meta_description: Amazon Web Services m4.xlarge is a general-purpose instance with 4 vCPUs, 16 GB RAM, and an Intel Xeon CPU, designed for legacy utility workloads.
tagline: A legacy general-purpose virtual machine offering a balanced memory-to-vCPU ratio for low-demand utility workloads and basic web serving.
bullet_points: Intel Xeon E5-2686 v4 processor with 4 dedicated vCPUs and 16.0 GB RAM
Xen hypervisor-based virtualization on x86_64 architecture
Baseline network bandwidth of 1 Gbps with no local storage
Consistently ranks in the bottom 25% of CPU and memory benchmarks
Suited for legacy applications, basic utility tasks, and low-traffic web servers
